steps.title: Lay out the two given facts
steps.content: (1) Twelve-day-old nestlings beg more in response to their own local dialect than to nonlocal dialects — an early preference for local song. (2) Song preference plays a role in mate selection. The completion must be the logical consequence of combining these two.

steps.title: Chain them forward
steps.content: Early preference for local dialect + song preference shapes mate choice $\Rightarrow$ when these nestlings mature, that preference should push them toward mates from their own local population rather than nonlocal ones.

steps.title: Eliminate by overreach or contradiction
steps.content:
• 'F. hypoleuca nestlings' preference for their own dialect likely disappears as they mature to promote socialization between different F. hypoleuca populations.': contradicts the inference — the whole reason the early preference matters is that it carries forward into mate selection. ✗
  • 'F. hypoleuca nestlings who show an early preference for their own dialect are likely to receive more food from their caretakers than nestlings who show no preferences among any F. hypoleuca dialects.': the study measured begging-call counts, not feeding outcomes; nothing in the passage links dialect preference to food rewards. Unsupported. ✗
  • 'F. hypoleuca nestlings' preference for their own dialect likely drives them when they mature to reproduce with other F. hypoleuca from local rather than nonlocal populations.': directly follows the chain — early local-dialect preference plus song-driven mate choice yields local mating. ✓
  • 'F. hypoleuca nestlings show a preference for both local F. hypoleuca dialects and the songs of other local songbirds over the songs of nonlocal birds of any species.': extends the finding to other species, which the study never tested. Overreach. ✗
That matches the correct answer — C is correct.
